runs.interpretation: Long-run CES elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in aggregate private-sector production in the U.S., defined as the percent change in the capital-labor ratio from a 1% change in the marginal rate of technical substitution between capital and labor.
citations: Arrow, Chenery, Minhas, and Solow (1961) CES/elasticity estimation ideas and interpretation
Griliches & Jorgenson (early–mid 1960s) capital-labor substitution evidence from growth-accounting frameworks
Standard neoclassical/production-function calibration literature suggesting elasticity typically around 0.5–1.5 for aggregate U.S. aggregates
runs.reasoningSummary: For U.S. aggregate private-sector production, I generally expect a moderate elasticity (close to, but not far above, Cobb–Douglas), with substantial uncertainty: values well below 1 are plausible if capital and labor are complements, while values above 1 are plausible if technology allows relatively easier substitution over the long run.

runs.interpretation: Long-run CES elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in U.S. aggregate private-sector production (elasticity parameter governing how the capital-labor ratio responds to changes in the marginal rate of technical substitution).
citations: CES production function / elasticity parameter interpretation (e.g., sigma as the capital-labor substitution elasticity)
Kaldor–Moore/Törnqvist-style growth accounting intuition and empirical dispersion across estimates
Evidence from macro/production-function identification debates affecting plausible ranges for sigma (often around 1, with substantial uncertainty)
runs.reasoningSummary: I view the U.S. aggregate private-sector elasticity as likely near unity (Cobb-Douglas-like) but with enough identification uncertainty and cross-study variability to allow values from moderately below 1 to around ~2 in the upper tail.

runs.interpretation: Long-run CES elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in U.S. aggregate private-sector production, i.e., the elasticity mapping a 1% change in the marginal rate of technical substitution (capital vs. labor) to a 1% change in the capital–labor ratio.

runs.interpretation: Long-run CES elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in U.S. aggregate private-sector production, measured as the elasticity governing how the capital-labor ratio responds to changes in the marginal rate of technical substitution.
citations: Solow/Swan growth accounting intuition on limited substitution over business cycles vs long run
Standard CES production function characterization and the role of the substitution parameter (sigma)
Empirical and calibration traditions using relatively moderate capital-labor substitution (often around 0.5–1)
runs.reasoningSummary: I place the median slightly below 1, reflecting the common view that capital and labor are not perfectly substitutable in aggregate production, while allowing for substantial uncertainty and potential higher values consistent with some empirical/cali­bration estimates.

runs.interpretation: Long-run CES elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in U.S. aggregate private-sector production, defined as the percent change in the capital–labor ratio per 1% change in the marginal rate of technical substitution.
citations: Hicksian/MRTS and CES production function elasticity relationship (sigma = 1/(1-rho))
Macro/production-function practice of using near-Cobb-Douglas or moderately substitutable CES (sigma around 0.5–1.5)
Common empirical sensitivity of factor substitution estimates and typical range implied by aggregate growth/accounting studies
runs.reasoningSummary: I center belief on moderate substitutability (near Cobb–Douglas but not fixed proportions). I put wider tails toward lower elasticity (more complementarity) and higher elasticity (closer to more flexible technologies), reflecting heterogeneity across methods and the challenge of identifying long-run factor substitution from aggregate data.
runs.rawResponse:
{
  "interpretation": "Long-run CES elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in U.S. aggregate private-sector production, defined as the percent change in the capital–labor ratio per 1% change in the marginal rate of technical substitution.",
  "point_estimate": 0.8,
  "quantiles": {
    "p05": 0.4,
    "p25": 0.65,
    "p50": 0.8,
    "p75": 1.05,
    "p95": 1.4
  },
  "citations": [
    "Hicksian/MRTS and CES production function elasticity relationship (sigma = 1/(1-rho))",
    "Macro/production-function practice of using near-Cobb-Douglas or moderately substitutable CES (sigma around 0.5–1.5)",
    "Common empirical sensitivity of factor substitution estimates and typical range implied by aggregate growth/accounting studies"
  ],
  "reasoning_summary": "I center belief on moderate substitutability (near Cobb–Douglas but not fixed proportions). I put wider tails toward lower elasticity (more complementarity) and higher elasticity (closer to more flexible technologies), reflecting heterogeneity across methods and the challenge of identifying long-run factor substitution from aggregate data."
}
